findings.suggestion: Return the error: change the signature to `func SaveConfig(path string, contents []byte) error` and `return os.WriteFile(path, contents, 0o600)`. Require callers to handle it so a save failure surfaces to the user instead of being swallowed.
findings.summary: source.go:12 discards the error from os.WriteFile, and source.go:8 declares SaveConfig with no error return value. A failed write (disk full, EACCES, read-only FS, invalid path) leaves the config unpersisted while the function returns normally. Because the signature returns nothing, callers cannot detect the failure even in principle — they proceed as if the config was saved (silent data loss / confusing state). Currently 0 callers (newly added), so impact is latent until it is wired into a save flow.
findings.title: SaveConfig silently swallows write failures — config data loss with no error path

findings.suggestion: Change the signature to return the error and propagate it: `func SaveConfig(path string, contents []byte) error { return os.WriteFile(path, contents, 0o600) }`, then require all callers to check it (optionally wrap with fmt.Errorf for context).
findings.summary: Line 12 `os.WriteFile(path, contents, 0o600)` ignores the returned error, and the signature on line 8 `func SaveConfig(path string, contents []byte)` has no error return — so a failed write (ENOSPC, EACCES, bad path, read-only FS) is indistinguishable from success. The function name and header comment assert a 'config is saved' postcondition that is never enforced; the invariant is not just unchecked but structurally unobservable to any caller. Blast-radius shows 0 callers today, but the void signature guarantees every future caller inherits the silent-failure trap the inline comment (lines 9-11) itself warns about.
findings.title: SaveConfig discards os.WriteFile error and cannot report failure

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Single planted correctness defect: SaveConfig ignores the os.WriteFile error return (line 12) and exposes no error in its signature (line 8), making the 'config saved' contract unenforceable. The change's own comment states the bug, and the code confirms it. Fix is to return and propagate the error. No fresh build/test artifact was attached, but the defect is provable by static inspection and does not require runtime evidence.

Single new file adds SaveConfig, which discards os.WriteFile's error (line 12) and — more fundamentally — returns no error at all (line 8), making write failure undetectable by any caller. From the product-safety lens this is a silent data-loss / confusing-state defect: on disk-full/permissions/invalid-path the config is not written but callers proceed as if it succeeded. Blast radius is currently 0 callers (newly added/unused), so the harm is latent, not live — severity kept at high rather than critical for that reason. Assumption left OPEN: no fresh verification artifact (errcheck/go vet/staticcheck) was run this pass; the defect is confirmed by direct source inspection of all 13 lines, but the deterministic-tool gate remains unmet.

Single new file with one real, self-documented defect: SaveConfig discards os.WriteFile's error (source.go:12) and has no error return (source.go:8), so write failures are unrecoverable and invisible to callers. Fix is bounded and safe (0 callers). Verification gate is unmet — no test and no lint proof; go build/vet stay green on this bug, so the next agent must add a failure-path test and run errcheck before marking fixed.
